jobs-1968-hewlett-call.summary_en: At twelve, Jobs needed parts to build a frequency counter, so he looked up HP co-founder Bill Hewlett's home number in the phone book and called him directly. Hewlett picked up, chatted with the boy for twenty minutes, mailed him the parts, and gave him a summer job on the HP assembly line. Jobs cited the episode for the rest of his life to make a single point: most people never get what they want because they never ask.

jobs-1972-09-reed-college.summary_en: In the fall of 1972 Jobs enrolled at Reed College in Oregon, an expensive liberal-arts school his adoptive parents spent their entire savings to afford. He dropped out after one semester but hung around campus for another eighteen months, auditing classes — most crucially a Western calligraphy course taught by Trappist monk Robert Palladino. A decade later, that course became the reason the Macintosh was the first mass-market computer with real typography.

jobs-1974-india.summary_en: Influenced by Reed alumnus Robert Friedland, Jobs and Daniel Kottke flew to India on a pilgrimage to the ashram of Neem Karoli Baba in the Kainchi hills. By the time they arrived, the guru had been dead since September 1973 and the ashram was nearly empty. The two spent seven months in India, shaving their heads, wearing local robes, and experiencing poverty and hunger firsthand. Jobs returned convinced that Eastern intuition was closer to truth than Western rationalism — a conviction that would shape his obsessive pursuit of simplicity and instinct in design.

jobs-1977-04-apple-ii.summary_en: The Apple II, unveiled at the West Coast Computer Faire, was one of the first successful mass-market personal computers and established Apple's early commercial foundation.

jobs-1980-12-12-apple-ipo.summary_en: Apple went public on NASDAQ at $22 per share, the largest IPO since Ford in 1956. The 25-year-old Jobs was instantly worth over $200 million.

jobs-1981-04-10-abc-nightline.summary_en: The 26-year-old Jobs sat down with Ted Koppel on ABC's flagship news program Nightline. Two months earlier he had also been filmed by ABC correspondent Bob Brown for a 20/20 segment that aired May 28. It was his first appearance as Apple's co-founder on mainstream network television, discussing how the personal computer would transform offices and homes.

jobs-1982-02-15-time-cover.summary_en: The 27-year-old Jobs landed on the cover of Time's February 15, 1982 issue for a feature titled "Striking It Rich," profiling a new generation of tech entrepreneurs. At year's end, Time prepared its "Person of the Year" and everyone expected Jobs — only for the magazine to name "The Computer" its "Machine of the Year" instead. Jobs broke down in tears.

jobs-1983-01-19-lisa.summary_en: Apple unveiled the Lisa at its annual shareholders' meeting — the first commercial personal computer with a graphical user interface, priced at $9,995. The Lisa was a commercial failure but defined the future of GUI-based desktop computing. Jobs himself had already been pushed off the Lisa team and was leading the smaller, cheaper Macintosh project.

jobs-1983-04-sculley.summary_en: Persuaded Pepsi CEO John Sculley to join Apple with the famous line: "Do you want to sell sugared water for the rest of your life, or do you want to come with me and change the world?" Two years later it would be Sculley who forced Jobs out of the company.

jobs-1983-06-aspen-idca.summary_en: The 28-year-old Jobs spoke at the 1983 International Design Conference in Aspen on the theme "the future isn't what it used to be." He made sweeping predictions about wireless networking, portable computers, and an app-store-like distribution model — all of which came true more than two decades later.

jobs-1985-02-playboy-interview.summary_en: A 12-page in-depth interview by David Sheff appeared in the February 1985 issue of Playboy, a year after the Mac launch and months before Jobs was forced out of Apple. He spoke candidly about everything from his childhood to product philosophy to Apple's internal politics. It remains the most revealing and information-dense interview from his early career.

jobs-1985-09-23-newsweek-rise-fall.summary_en: Six days after submitting his resignation, Jobs sat with Newsweek editors Gerald C. Lubenow and Michael Rogers for three and a half hours, walking through his rise and fall at Apple. He reflected on his mistakes during his final two years at the company, assessed Sculley, and outlined his plans for NeXT. It was his first systematic public statement after leaving Apple.

jobs-1988-10-12-next-computer.summary_en: Jobs held a lavish launch event at San Francisco's Davies Symphony Hall for the NeXT Computer, attended by more than 3,000 people. The two-hour show included a product demo and a live performance in which the NeXT Cube played a Bach duet with a symphony violinist. Tim Berners-Lee built the first web browser and server on a NeXT Cube.

jobs-1990-pbs-machine-that-changed-world.summary_en: In early 1990, the 35-year-old Jobs gave a rare extended interview for WGBH's five-part PBS documentary The Machine That Changed the World. Jobs reflected on the rise of the personal computer, Apple's early days, being ousted, and his thinking about the future of computing — one of his few long-form public statements during the NeXT period.

jobs-1991-03-18-marries-laurene.summary_en: Jobs married Laurene Powell in a Zen Buddhist ceremony at the Ahwahnee Hotel in Yosemite National Park, officiated by the Japanese Zen master Kobun Chino Otogawa. About fifty guests attended, including his adoptive father Paul Jobs and his biological sister Mona Simpson. The wedding cake was vegan, shaped like Yosemite's Half Dome.

jobs-1992-04-mit-sloan.summary_en: In the spring of 1992 Jobs delivered a 72-minute guest lecture at MIT's Sloan School of Management — his most complete business-school talk during the NeXT years. He discussed his contempt for management consultants, his philosophy of hiring, reflections on being ousted from Apple, and why the world still needed a new computer company.

jobs-1994-06-16-rolling-stone-goodell.summary_en: With NeXT already out of the hardware business and Pixar not yet redeemed by Toy Story, Jobs gave a long interview to Rolling Stone reporter Jeff Goodell. It came during one of the deepest troughs of his career, yet the conversation revealed sharp judgment on object-oriented programming, the future of the internet, and why Apple had failed.

jobs-1995-lost-interview.summary_en: A 70-minute raw interview filmed for the PBS documentary Triumph of the Nerds, of which only about 10 minutes made the final cut. The master tape was lost in the mail for years until a VHS backup was found in the director's garage and released as a standalone documentary in 2012. Jobs spoke with extraordinary candor about why Apple died, the soul of a product, and the clash between marketing culture and engineering culture.

jobs-1995-04-20-smithsonian-oral-history.summary_en: The Smithsonian's Computerworld Awards Program sent its executive director Daniel Morrow to record a formal oral-history video interview with Jobs. Conducted for the museum's permanent archive — it remains one of the most complete primary-source accounts of Jobs' early life and formation.

jobs-1995-11-29-pixar-ipo.summary_en: One week after Toy Story opened, Pixar went public on NASDAQ. Shares opened at $22 and closed at $39, valuing the company at $1.5 billion. Jobs, holding 80%, went overnight from financial distress to being a billionaire at 40. It was Pixar, not Apple, that actually made Steve Jobs a billionaire.

jobs-1996-02-wired-interview.summary_en: The cover interview for Wired's February 1996 issue caught Jobs in the final stretch of the NeXT era. He declared that "the desktop computer industry is dead" and that "innovation has virtually ceased," predicting that the next wave would center on the Web — and that "the heart of the Web will be commerce." Every one of those predictions came true.

jobs-1997-09-think-different-internal.summary_en: Days before the Think Different campaign went public, Jobs gave an internal presentation to Apple employees unveiling the new brand strategy. Originally private, the footage leaked online years later and is widely regarded as his most candid masterclass in marketing. His core argument: marketing is about values, not features; great brands are about what you believe, not what you make.

jobs-1997-09-28-think-different.summary_en: Apple launched the iconic "Think Different" advertising campaign — black-and-white footage of Einstein, Gandhi, John Lennon, Picasso, Martin Luther King Jr., and other "crazy ones." The copy, "Here's to the crazy ones," was written by TBWA/Chiat/Day's Rob Siltanen and Lee Clow, with Jobs personally involved in the final edit.

jobs-1998-05-06-imac-unveiled.summary_en: At De Anza College's Flint Center, Jobs pulled back a black cloth to reveal the original iMac: "iMac comes from the marriage of the excitement of the Internet with the simplicity of Macintosh." The translucent Bondi-blue shell, designed by Jonathan Ive, dropped the floppy drive, built in USB, and adopted an all-in-one form factor. It sold 278,000 units in six weeks — saving a company on the brink of bankruptcy.

jobs-1998-05-25-businessweek.summary_en: A year after his return to Apple, Jobs sat down with BusinessWeek reporter Andy Reinhardt. The interview came weeks after the iMac reveal, at the exact inflection point when Apple was turning from losses to profit. Jobs explained why "simple can be harder than complex" and why "you can't design products by focus groups."

jobs-1998-08-15-imac.summary_en: The first product Jobs personally shepherded after his return to Apple. Its translucent Bondi-blue shell, all-in-one design, removal of the floppy drive, and built-in USB redefined the consumer PC aesthetic and marked the beginning of Apple's renaissance.

jobs-2000-01-05-permanent-ceo.summary_en: At the Macworld San Francisco 2000 keynote, Jobs announced he was dropping the "interim" from his title — officially becoming Apple's CEO after two and a half years. The same presentation unveiled Mac OS X. Two weeks later the board approved 10 million stock options and a Gulfstream V jet as compensation for saving the company.

jobs-2001-05-19-first-apple-store.summary_en: Apple's first two retail stores opened simultaneously at Tysons Corner, Virginia, and Glendale, California. Industry conventional wisdom called it a folly — analyst David Goldstein publicly predicted the Apple Store would fold within two years. On opening day 500 people lined up before dawn; over the first weekend the two stores drew 7,700 visitors and $599,000 in revenue.

jobs-2003-04-28-itunes-music-store.summary_en: Apple launched the iTunes Music Store, the first legal digital music store backed by all five major record labels. Songs cost 99 cents each, with no subscription. The store reshaped the entire music industry: piracy declined, artists began selling individual tracks instead of being locked into album bundles. It sold one million songs in its first week.

jobs-2003-10-cancer-diagnosis.summary_en: A routine CT scan in October revealed a shadow on Jobs' pancreas. A biopsy confirmed an islet cell tumor — a much rarer, slower-growing, and surgically curable form of pancreatic cancer than the common adenocarcinoma. But Jobs refused immediate surgery, spending nine months pursuing alternative therapies. That nine-month delay became the subject of intense medical controversy, with many arguing it was the critical reason he ultimately died.

jobs-2003-60-minutes.summary_en: A rare long-form TV interview with CBS's 60 Minutes. Jobs spoke about being fired from Apple, the origins of Pixar, his product philosophy, and his view of teams versus individual heroes. It was his first serious sit-down with a major US television network after returning to Apple.

jobs-2003-12-rolling-stone-in-conversati.summary_en: Jeff Goodell returned to Rolling Stone nine years after his first Jobs interview, this time focused on the music industry eight months after the iTunes Music Store launch. Jobs blasted subscription-based music services as "a bankrupt business model" and explained why porting iTunes to Windows was essential.

jobs-2004-07-26-newsweek-ipod-nation.summary_en: Newsweek's senior tech writer Steven Levy put Jobs on the cover for the "iPod Nation" feature. Jobs told Levy: "I was walking down Madison Avenue and every other block had someone wearing white earbuds, and I thought, 'Oh my God, it's starting to happen.'" The interview took place days before Jobs' Whipple surgery; at the time of the cover shoot he already knew his diagnosis but had told no one.

jobs-2004-08-01-whipple-surgery.summary_en: After nine months of alternative therapies, Jobs finally agreed to surgery. On July 31 he underwent a Whipple procedure at Stanford University Medical Center. Afterward he emailed the entire Apple staff: "This weekend I underwent a successful surgery to remove a cancerous tumor from my pancreas." At the time, both he and the public believed the surgery was curative; it later became clear the cancer had already spread.

jobs-2006-01-24-disney-pixar.summary_en: Newly installed Disney CEO Bob Iger pushed through the acquisition as a bet on rebooting Disney Animation. Each share of Pixar was exchanged for 2.3 shares of Disney, valuing the deal at roughly $7.4 billion. Jobs became Disney's largest individual shareholder (about 7%) and joined the Disney board.

jobs-2007-05-30-d5-gates.summary_en: Walt Mossberg and Kara Swisher brought Jobs and Bill Gates together on the same stage at the fifth All Things Digital conference — the only time the two rivals shared a stage in over thirty years. The 90-minute conversation included mutual tributes. The interview came one month after the iPhone announcement and one month before its launch.

jobs-2007-11-time-invention-of-year.summary_en: Time magazine named the original iPhone its 2007 Invention of the Year. Lev Grossman's cover feature included a deep interview with Jobs and chronicled the iPhone team's two-year secret development process and the failed ROKR collaboration with Motorola. It remains the most authoritative contemporaneous account of how the iPhone went from concept to product.

jobs-2008-01-15-macbook-air.summary_en: The famous "envelope moment" — Jobs walked to the side of the stage, slid a MacBook Air out of an ordinary manila inter-office envelope, and proved it was thin enough to fit inside. The world's first ultrabook was born.

jobs-2008-03-06-iphone-sdk.summary_en: At Apple's Town Hall auditorium, Jobs announced iPhone 2.0 software, the iPhone SDK, and the App Store. For just $99 a year, any developer could get the SDK and distribute apps wirelessly to users worldwide. It was a triumph of platform thinking: Apple transformed the iPhone from a standalone phone into a developer ecosystem. The App Store launched four months later with 500 apps.

jobs-2009-01-05-medical-leave.summary_en: Jobs published an open letter on Apple's website acknowledging that his continuing weight loss was caused by a "hormone imbalance" and that he needed a leave of absence. The reality was far more serious: he was awaiting a liver transplant. On March 21 he underwent the transplant at Methodist University Hospital in Memphis.

jobs-2010-04-29-thoughts-on-flash.summary_en: Jobs published a signed open letter on Apple's website laying out six reasons why iPhone, iPad, and iPod touch would never support Adobe Flash: it was proprietary, web video had moved to H.264, it drained battery life, was not built for touch, was the top security vulnerability source, and inserted a third-party layer between the OS and developers. The letter accelerated Flash's decline. In 2020 Flash was formally retired.

jobs-2010-06-01-d8.summary_en: Three months after the iPad launch, Jobs returned to the eighth All Things Digital conference for a 90-minute live interview. Topics ranged from the Flash standoff to Apple's deteriorating relationship with Google. The famous analogy — "PCs are going to be like trucks; iPads are going to be like cars" — came from this conversation. It was his last major public interview.

jobs-2010-06-07-iphone-4.summary_en: At the WWDC 2010 keynote Jobs unveiled the iPhone 4, calling it "the biggest leap since the original iPhone." New features included the Retina Display (326 ppi), FaceTime video calling, and the Apple-designed A4 chip. Two weeks after launch, users discovered that certain grips shorted the steel antenna band and caused signal loss — touching off "Antennagate."

jobs-2010-07-16-antennagate.summary_en: A month after the iPhone 4 launch, its steel antenna band's tendency to short-circuit when gripped had become a full-blown crisis. Consumer Reports refused to recommend the phone. Jobs called an emergency press conference, personally demonstrated the signal issue, argued that "all phones have this problem," and announced free bumper cases for every iPhone 4 owner. One of the few times in his career he faced a PR crisis head-on.

jobs-2011-06-06-wwdc-icloud.summary_en: Jobs' final Apple keynote. He was visibly gaunt but insisted on taking the stage to introduce Mac OS X Lion, iOS 5, and iCloud. iCloud represented his last strategic pivot for Apple: from "the Mac as the digital hub" to "the cloud is the hub, and every device is just a window into it." Three days later he presented the Apple Park proposal to the Cupertino City Council — his last public appearance.

jobs-2011-06-07-cupertino-city-council.summary_en: Jobs personally presented Apple's new campus proposal to the Cupertino City Council — a ring-shaped main building housing 12,000 employees, promptly dubbed "the spaceship." He told the council: "It's a little like a spaceship landed." This was his final public appearance; he died five months later. The video was permanently archived by the City of Cupertino.

musk-1999-03-x-com-founding.summary_en: Using $12 million from his Zip2 payout, Musk founded X.com with the goal of building a fully digital bank. It was the first time he used the letter X as a company name — a habit he would return to repeatedly.

musk-2000-03-paypal-merger.summary_en: X.com merged with Confinity, the company founded by Peter Thiel and Max Levchin. Confinity's email-payments product was called PayPal, which became the flagship. Musk served as CEO but his push to migrate from Unix to Microsoft led the board to oust him six months later while he was on his honeymoon. Peter Thiel took over as CEO.

musk-2004-02-tesla-chairman.summary_en: Tesla had been founded by Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning in July 2003. In February 2004 Musk led the $7.5 million Series A round, becoming the company's largest shareholder and chairman. He was not Tesla's founder, but from the A round onward he was deeply involved in product decisions. During the 2008 financial crisis he would escalate to CEO.

musk-2008-10-tesla-ceo.summary_en: By October 2008, Tesla was on the verge of collapse. The financial crisis had frozen capital markets, Roadster production was badly behind schedule, and Tesla had only weeks of cash left. Musk removed founding CEO Martin Eberhard and took the role himself. SpaceX was simultaneously on the brink — Falcon 1 had succeeded just ten days earlier. Musk poured his last personal funds into both companies.

musk-2022-04-25-twitter-agreement.summary_en: After months of public sparring, Twitter's board unanimously accepted Musk's offer of $54.20 per share, totaling roughly $44 billion. Musk was already Twitter's largest individual shareholder with about 9% of the stock. It was only the beginning — months of attempted withdrawal, lawsuits, and a forced settlement followed before the deal closed in October.
